item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP deserves to be ranked higher. Writing is neat and the author seems to have solid ground and grasp on the idea.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe if he watched Trump's inauguration, we can finally clear up the "alternative facts" thing.

item.title: How would his forgetting affect the info in SCP-152?
item.content:encoded: How would his forgetting affect the info in SCP-152?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept here is sound, but it's executed in an extremely confusing way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the public know about the Foundation now that SCP-2032 is taking residence there and thus will be remembering his time there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why not "it" or "they"?

E: I did not realize this was a month old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the concept, two minor things before I upvote:

1. Alzheimers is a form of dementia, he could of course have co-morbid vascular dementia but to say he has alzheimers AND dementia doesn't make sense.

2. The phrase "exceedingly correct" doesn't work. Maybe you meant exceedingly accurate?

item.title: Excellent spin on this concept
item.content:encoded:
I think that you've really portrayed the idea of this SCP as a 'final historian' in a very interesting way, however some tone issues and grammar issues just need to be cleaned up. At times I feel that you've been drawn away from the clinical tone that is the standard, however generally you communicate this idea very well.

A gold sticker is hereby awarded to you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clarified and dated

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Immediately following the Burning Monk at the apex of public opposition towards the Vietnam War, SCP-2032 had a moderate stroke…

That's not a very clinical sentence, and stood out to me. '…the Burning Monk' would be 'the self-immolation of Buddhist monk Thích Quảng Đức', or something to that effect, because the monk in question has a name, there were actually several self-immolations by Buddhist monks during that period, and calling the event 'the Burning Monk' is grammatically confused and sounds strange. Also, is there any way other than narrative fiat that the Foundation could know that the stroke actually had any connection to Quang Duc's death? Why not include the date of the stroke?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept was a bit ambitious, but I think you pulled it off well enough. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty good skip. It seems to me there have been an extremely large amount of skips in the 2030s being deleted due to poor quality, so congrats on breaking what I refer to as the 2030 curse.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, it depends. In a lot of situations, not using "he" or "she" is just plain awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
he

him

his

This seems rather inappropriate to me. SCP objects are SCP objects, not SCP people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm, I'm not really a fan, to be honest. I feel like this article goes into a lot more detail than the core concept really needs, and that sort of bogs it down. As for that core concept, there's nothing wrong with "a man whose memories affect the historical record" but it doesn't really grab my attention. If this were shorter I'd probably neutral vote, but as-is I'm going to have to downvote this. It's not bad, but it spends a lot of time elaborating on a concept that just isn't strong enough to support the article. In particular, I felt like the interview didn't add anything to the article as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly; Alzheimer's and strokes are not solely related to aging. They both have numerous genetic factors which could make them happen early in life. The stroke is elaborated on as being caused due to a widescale global outcry towards a drastic historical event which has little similar enough to compare to it.

Secondly; Mental illness is not quite the same as physical illness, which is being referred to in that quote in context.

Thirdly; Yes, it actually could happen in theory if his DNA no longer shortened but instead reached a point of perfectly replicating itself. It's plausible, anomalous yes but still plausible, he would no longer age in the sense that we think of it. We die of old age and the illnesses that come with it due to the telomeres at the end of our DNA being skipped over numerous times as our cells divide, it eventually gets too faulty to remain stable. He's reached cellular equilibrium.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Following a mild stroke SCP-2032 is on a mechanical ventilation apparatus and is confined to a wheel chair

This, plus the Alzheimer's, would indicate to me that he does have some "organ issues or other illnesses pertaining to aging besides arthritis," no? And maybe some other members with a bio or med background can confirm/refute this, but if something ages, doesn't that necessarily imply that the aging process will reach a point where life cannot continue? Does it make any theoretical sense for something to age to a point and then just stop forever?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe they're only immortal until they settle down and have a new kid.

Maybe he hasn't because he doesn't want another to bear his burden.

who knows except for me :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of human history is forgotten. What if that's because this guy's predecessors died? This guy dies, someone new like him is born, humanity has to start again, and it goes like that forever.
My interpretation of it, if the author doesn't downright shoot this down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But statistically there's more information to say he will continue to live sooner than he will die. They can test numerous factors, including the remaining length of his DNA for signs of degradation associated with aging. We die when we run out, if his dna can replicate continuously without shortening it is plausible he can age without dying

edit; I have added a paragraph to state this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation can't possibly know that he can't die of old age. It is impossible to prove a negative. Just because he hasn't yet, doesn't mean he never will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, because he has apparent inhuman longevity, that is he's lived for thousands of years if not more, so the immediate problem is his mind. He doesn't appear to have any organ issues or other illnesses pertaining to aging besides arthritis (which can be genetic) and his mental health problems. He can't die of old age.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll be honest, this actually strikes me as too consequential. I mean, he is going to die, right? That's inevitable. And he may lose his mind even sooner. So what we have is something essentially uncontainable. The world will lose its history, and there's nothing we can do about it. Am I wrong?

item.title: Re: Thaumiel to Keter
item.content:encoded:
Ok, but now the footnote makes no sense.

Edit: And you fixed it. Ok.

item.title: Thaumiel to Keter
item.content:encoded: Classification changed due to several mentions that the potential risk outweighed the active benefits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat concept. However, this is the rare instance where I think this humanoid should be deemed Keter, primarily because of its potential to erase recorded history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an interesting concept, but I'm just not onboard with the idea of thaumiel-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think he's classified as Thaumiel because he's helping to maintain public memory, and the records in the safe zone help the Foundation know what's true and what isn't. Also, because they blank his memory after containment breaches.

item.title: SCP-2032
item.content:encoded: Thanks to AndarielHalo for help with the dialog, and frankhlane for helping with a lot of technical feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't this be classed as keter rather than safe, because of the consqences of him aging having to be contained? If this thing can destroy history and there is no way to stop it except by keeping his memory intact then I think it deserves keter. and I am not entirely sure if his abilities are quite useful enough to classify as thaumiel. It is a really interesting idea and it is decently well written, but I think you need to expand more on some parts of it and fix some inconsistencies like it being classed as safe or thaumiel when it should be keter because of the disastrous effect he is having on history.

I have to go to work now. I think I will explain a few other problems I had with this later if somebody else does not point them out.
